meta.title: Mercedes E-Class W213 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.h1: Mercedes E-Class W213 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the tenth-generation E-Class saloon — from the M274 four-cylinder petrol to the M256 inline-six and the OM654 diesel that redefined the executive segment. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the 48V EQ Boost mild-hybrid technology that set a new benchmark. Backed by 1,250+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes E-Class W213 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W213 specifically — including the pre- and post-facelift changes that introduced the M264 and M256 EQ Boost families.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: * OM654.920: 95% confidence — inherited from the Model Page's verified figures for the wider Mercedes range. * M274.910/920: 90% confidence — well‑documented M274; pattern data. * M264.920: 85% confidence — well‑regarded M264; pattern data. * M256.930: 90% confidence — well‑documented Mercedes inline-six; pattern data. * M276.853: 85% confidence — well‑known V6; pattern data. * OM656.929: 80% confidence — inline-six diesel; pattern data. * OM642.855: 80% confidence — V6 diesel with known issues; provisional.

overview.intro: The W213 is the tenth generation of the Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class, launched in 2016 as the successor to the W212. Built on the Mercedes MRA platform, it introduced a new range of engines including the M274 four-cylinder petrol, the M276 V6 petrol, the OM654 four-cylinder diesel, and the OM642 V6 diesel. A significant facelift arrived in 2019, bringing new M264 four-cylinder petrol and M256 inline-six petrol engines with 48V EQ Boost mild-hybrid technology, alongside the OM656 inline-six diesel. Production ended in 2023, succeeded by the W214.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes MRA (shared with S‑Class W222, CLS C257) • Predecessor: W212 (2009–2016) • Successor: W214 (2023–present) • Sibling body styles in this generation: S213 (Estate), C238 (E‑Class Coupé), A238 (E‑Class Cabriolet)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W213 E‑Class generated 1,250+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM654 diesel as the most‑requested powertrain.

items.quote: Mercedes' own 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder diesel, introduced in 2016, is the pick of the W213 diesel range — efficient, refined, and backed by a strong reliability record when serviced properly. It's 17% lighter than the OM651 it replaced.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a modern, efficient diesel for high‑mileage executive motoring.

items.quote: Mercedes' 3.0‑litre inline‑six petrol, with 367 bhp and EQ Boost mild‑hybrid assistance, delivers effortless power and silky refinement. It's the defining engine of the W213 generation.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the full executive saloon experience.

items.quote: The V6 diesel is generally reliable but its oil cooler seals can fail, leaking oil into the V of the engine — a labour‑intensive repair. The OM642 was phased out in favour of the OM656 inline-six for good reason.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 diesel refinement but can afford the premium maintenance.

items.engine: M256.930 (E 450 / AMG E 53) — EQ Boost system and turbo
items.quote: The 48V mild‑hybrid system and turbocharger on the M256 can be costly to repair if issues arise. The AMG E 53's higher output adds further complexity.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want AMG performance and can afford the premium running costs.

items.quote: The mid‑range diesel offers the best balance of performance (194 bhp), economy (up to 60 mpg), and reliability. It's the sweet spot in the W213 range.

The 2.0‑litre four‑cylinder petrol with 48V mild hybrid delivers 299 bhp + 14 hp boost and genuine performance (0‑62 mph in 5.9 seconds). It's often overlooked in favour of the six‑cylinder but offers a compelling blend of performance and efficiency.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol power without the six‑cylinder running costs.

rows.commonMajorFailure: EQ Boost system, turbo wear, coolant leaks
rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol — silky inline‑six, premium costs.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Turbo wear, cooling system, EQ Boost
rows.ownershipVerdict: AMG Tax — high performance, high running costs.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Oil cooler seal leak, swirl flap motor
rows.ownershipVerdict: Avoid — V6 diesel; phased out for good reason.

economicsRule.title: The W213 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,800–£4,800 OM654 recon against a £10,000–£30,000 W213 sits at 9–48% of the car's value — comfortably within our 70% Mercedes threshold, making replacement a sensible decision. Against a higher‑mileage E 350 d with the OM656, the same maths is still favourable (11–55%). The W213's strong retained values make most engine replacements economically viable.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W213 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 220 d (OM654) — best balance of economy and reliability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W213 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: AMG E 53 (M256) — high‑performance running costs add up.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W213 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 220 d (OM654) — the sweet spot in the range.

problems.title: OM654 Injector Seal Leaks (Black Death)
problems.rootCause: The injector seals on the OM654 can fail, allowing combustion gases to escape past the injector. This creates a carbon buildup that sounds like a ticking noise and can eventually damage the cylinder head.
problems.recommendation: If you hear a ticking noise from the engine bay, have the injector seals checked immediately — a £400–£1,000 repair is far cheaper than the £2,800–£4,800 replacement that follows if the head is damaged.

cta.label: Compare OM654 engine prices for your W213 diesel →

problems.title: M256 EQ Boost System & Coolant Leaks
problems.rootCause: The M256's 48V mild‑hybrid system and coolant system components can develop issues over time — water pump failures and coolant leaks are the most common reported problems.
problems.recommendation: On E 450 and AMG E 53 models, have the coolant system and EQ Boost components inspected at 60,000 miles — a £600–£1,200 repair is cheap insurance against a £3,500–£6,500 engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare M256 engine prices for your W213 petrol →

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leak (pre‑2018 E 350 d)
problems.rootCause: The OM642's oil cooler seals (located in the V of the engine) can degrade and leak, allowing oil to mix with coolant or leak onto the engine. Repair requires significant disassembly.
problems.recommendation: If you notice oil in the coolant or a burning oil smell on a pre‑2018 E 350 d, have the oil cooler seals inspected immediately — an £800–£1,500 repair is far cheaper than the £3,200–£5,500 replacement that follows if the engine overheats.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your W213 V6 diesel →

problems.title: OM656 AdBlue/SCR System Issues
problems.rootCause: The OM656's AdBlue and SCR (selective catalytic reduction) system can suffer from sensor failures and crystallisation, leading to warning lights and potential limp‑home mode.
problems.recommendation: If your E 350 d or E 400 d shows an AdBlue warning, have the system inspected immediately — a £400–£1,000 repair is far cheaper than the £3,200–£5,500 replacement that follows if the system fails catastrophically.

cta.label: Compare OM656 engine prices for your W213 diesel →

replacementCosts.h2: W213 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — the W213 shares its MRA platform with the CLS (C257) and S‑Class (W222), so engine‑bay access and labour times are consistent across the range. Costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W213 saloon only. The S213 estate, C238 coupé and A238 cabriolet share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style.
